channel.title: Vinny Seeram
channel.description: Recent content in on Vinny Seeram

item.title: OTUS Acronyms are out of hand
item.author: vinnyseeram@vinnyseeram.com (Vinny Seeram)
item.description:
It’s time to pull it back on OTUS ("_ Of the United States") acronyms. We’ve simply gone too far.

POTUS - I’m okay with this. Has a faux-Latin feel to it, and western politicians do like to cosplay Roman Republic.

FLOTUS - Does have an elegant, floral ring to it. When we have a First Gentleman it will become dangerously close to a homophobic slur.

SCOTUS - Sounds like a venereal disease.

VPOTUS - This is just obscene, completely aura-less. Somehow worse than “Veep.”

BRUTUS - This is not an acronym, this is the Roman Senator that betrayed Julius Caesar.

COTUS - This one stands for the Constitution of the United States. Obvious coitus joke aside, this is the syndrome that results from untreated SCOTUS.

SLOTUS - You’re just messing with me now.

item.title: Why I always order a Tom Collins
item.author: vinnyseeram@vinnyseeram.com (Vinny Seeram)
item.description:
Most of my favorite things are not based on their objective quality or a meaningful personal story. I’m a creature of habit, more than anything. As someone from New York, I’m semi-frequently asked about my favorite slice of pizza in the city. My answer is honest and dreadfully boring; “Whatever’s down the street.” What was down the street when I was a kid was a place named “Luigi’s Pizza” that was owned by Albanians. I don’t think it’ll show up on any TimeOut lists, but it was a reliable simple pleasure.

One of my other favorite simple pleasures in life is to walk into a moderately populated bar (enough people to be lively, but not so much as to be chaotic) and order a Tom Collins. A little while ago I asked an older friend of mine if they often made them while bartending in the 90s. “A Tom Collins is easy,” he said. “That’s Gin and Tom Collins mix.”

If I ever record an album, “Gin and Tom Collins Mix” is going into the shortlist of potential titles. Life was probably better when you could walk into a grocery store and find Collins mix next to the club soda. Anyhow, nowadays we’re barbarically forced to make a Tom Collins using the following ingredients:

  • 2 oz gin
  • 1 oz lemon juice (NOT lime juice)
  • 1 oz simple syrup
  • topped with club soda
  • garnished with a lemon wedge, maybe a maraschino cherry too if you’re feeling spicy

So is there a special reason why it’s my go to? It’s an anachronism in a world where the act of drinking alcohol itself is falling out of fashion with my Gen Z peers. In a landscape more commonly filled by Old Fashioneds and Espresso Martinis, it’s often an inconvenience to the bartender to order this arcane highball. I have given many sheepish shrugs to overworked, exasperated bartenders pulling out their phone to ask Google (or, god forbid, ChatGPT) how to make the old man bullshit I just ordered.

Maybe it’s because the drink was once the subject of a sort of “viral prank” in 1874. It went something like this:

“Hey, have you heard of Tom Collins?”

“No. Who is that?”

“He’s in that bar talking about you.”

“What the heck, I’d better take fisticuffs to that rapscallion.”

The latter gentlemen would then enter the bar inquiring about Tom Collins, only to be served the drink. Everyone would share a hearty guffaw before returning to work in the coal mines and dying an early death from black lung.

Maybe the drink is really good? It’s pretty good, essentially a bubbly alcoholic lemonade. Very crisp and refreshing at the end of a long day. If we’re being real though, a whiskey sour is probably better.

There’s nothing whimsical here; it’s a simple and inoffensive drink, and ordering it has become a habit. Favorites are rarely momentous or even objectively the best, but rather they are the things we settle into and find comfort with. Moreover, while it is important to celebrate and strive for perfection, I think it is equally important to celebrate that which is “good enough.” And for me, few things capture “good enough” better than enjoying Italian food made by Balkans and washing it down with a forgotten 19th century highball.

item.title: Contact Me
item.author: vinnyseeram@vinnyseeram.com (Vinny Seeram)
item.description:
For all inquiries, you can email me at vinnyseeram@gmail.com.

If you’d like to receive email updates for upcoming shows or blog content, you can join my mailing list using the form below.

item.title: About
item.author: vinnyseeram@vinnyseeram.com (Vinny Seeram)
item.description:
Vinny Seeram is a stand-up comedian based in New York City. His draws from his experiences growing up as a second generation Indo-Guyanese immigrant in the Bronx, and his observations on politics, race, and culture.

Vinny performs all over New York, most frequently at his home club Q.E.D. Astoria, where he hosts a weekly open mic and his comedy showcase show, vinnydotcomedy. He has also been featured in festivals across the United States, including:

  • Boston Comedy Festival (Boston, MA)
  • Vice City Comedy Festival (Miami, FL)
  • Mountain Fresh Comedy Festival (Colorado)
  • Crooked Mouth Comedy Festival (Endicott, NY)
  • Comedy Mob Festival (New York, NY), Semi-finalist
  • HaHa Harvest Comedy Festival (Jersey City, NJ)
  • Salem Comedy and Spirits Festival (Salem, MA)
  • Last Word Comedy Festival (Williamsburg, VA)

Vinny has also appeared as an actor in several theatrical productions in New York. In AfterWork Theater’s productions of You Can’t Take it With You and The Crucible he played Tony Kirby and John Proctor, respectively. He has most recently appeared in ScriptClub NYC’s production of The 39 Steps as The Professor and other supporting roles.
